item.title: Intesa’s bid for MPS restores some sanity to Italy’s M&A scene
item.description: An Intesa victory might be appealing to Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni

item.title: The incredible shrinking influence of Putin’s Russia
item.description: Armenia is the latest country to leave Moscow’s orbit

item.title: We need to learn how to argue with AI
item.description: Putting humans in the loop is pointless if they simply rubber-stamp authoritative-sounding information

item.title: America’s nostalgia for a Cuba that no longer exists
item.description: Every year, Miami recreates Havana as a pristine paradise lost in the revolution

item.title: Why private equity in football can still be a winning proposition
item.description: Chelsea demonstrates that investment in the sport has merit but requires patience

item.title: Netanyahu’s grand strategy is coming apart
item.description: The Israeli leader has tried to secure his country by entirely military means. It isn’t working.

item.title: Did jobs just kill the bull market?
item.description: The AI trade meets higher rates

item.title: The World Cup offers pubs a chance to change the story
item.description: Tournament gives the industry a chance to pull in more punters and pull more pints

item.title: A ‘big bang’ reversal of Brexit is both unrealistic and unnecessary
item.description: Going back would create some big new problems but Switzerland provides an alternative model

item.title: We must not grant AI agents legal personhood
item.description: What kind of sanctions could keep a non-human corporation in check?

item.title: SpaceX IPO will not break capital markets but add to strains
item.description: Offering will widen the valuation gap between the constituents of indices and the companies outside them

item.title: How to chair a board in the post-governance age
item.description: BP and SpaceX show transatlantic views of the role are very far apart

item.title: The strange case of who chooses the next Labour prime minister
item.description: Around three-quarters of those who can vote in any contest on who succeeds Starmer are not Labour Party members

item.title: Are we at the start of a new investment super-cycle?
item.description: AI, clean energy and defence spending are reinforcing each other, amplifying potential spend

item.title: The coming rise of anti-AI populism
item.description: Anxiety about the technology is set to generate a political backlash

item.title: Britain’s questionable reliance on Palantir
item.description: Government should use the best technology for any task, but be wary of ‘lock-ins’

item.title: Expensive oil is making electric vehicles look positively cheap
item.description: Petrol retails for £1.58 a litre in the UK, up about 20% since the start of the year

item.title: We are optimising ourselves to death
item.description: A tracker might tell you that you got to bed too late, but it doesn’t tell you that you were busy living your life

item.title: We should be getting better at AI by now
item.description: From cancelled novels to legal fines, the scale of blunders only halfway through the year suggests the opposite

item.title: Pricing the oil inventory drawdown
item.description: Could Brent crude hit $140 per barrel?

item.title: Investors wake up to the merits of sleep biotech
item.description: More people worry about poor sleep than excess weight, but drugmakers have been deterred from entering the market by costs

item.title: On yer bike! Freud and middle-aged men in Lycra
item.description: The ever-present fear of ridicule among cycling’s weekend warriors tells us a thing or two about human nature

item.title: In World Cup season, choose your financial trophies carefully
item.description: Real Madrid’s president has made it very clear that investing in the club is mainly about having fun and making contacts

item.title: Wasting China’s solar panel surplus is madness
item.description: Clean power is within our reach — yet factories sit idle

item.title: Can AI save a company’s soul?
item.description: It is easy to imagine institutional memory lost to automation preserved in silicon — but there is one crucial difference
